feedback.text: I'm going to add my experience on each Digital Fairness Fitness Check I had with each statement: 1. Talking about the online interfaces, there were times on online shops that they will put abusive pop-up's to push you fake "limited offers". This got into a point that you can experience the same thing watching advertisements on Instagram(Meta) or YouTube(Alphabet) which also doesn't bother even to moderate the advertisements, from overseas tech giants and using little influencers to push referrals. I'm having doubts if that big tech giant is paying those advertisements using those influencers instead of it's normal brand. Finally, these tactics are usually used by scammers with ease and be effective. Why check if the site is legit if you are on a hurry? When the UX/UI design is only to make people spend even to not get bothered by advertisements and make more consumer retention. There is something terrible behind it. 2. Gambling-like tactics are everywhere, from most of the "free" video-games on the smart-phone marketplaces, to even online shopping. Some publishers even hired psychology experts to pray in the consumer, and even worse when you can't see how many real money are you spending. Or how difficult they make to see even the odds of what are you gambling. Here I want the people in charge to look up, the many documentaries of the Counter Strike: Global Offensive(CS:GO), renamed CS:2. That tackles on the black economy behind trading and gambling with that game's weapon skins. And how unmoderated is ROBLOX, on how anyone can put gambling systems easy to find for children in the games created by the community. 3. This is a problem, when RTVE(Public State Television) is warning everyone in Spain about the personalised targeting tactics used by online travel agencies and airlines are using. They recommend to search in incognito mode just to get rid of the marketing cookies, getting even higher price if we look the same flight again. It's usual to discuss something or even talk about "x" topic, and have the sensation of being watched by your phone or something like that, when you even have a target advertisement about that conversation. Everyone usually joke about it, until you read some news about Alexa(Amazon) recording private conversations. Going a bit beyond, these excessive user monitoring and tracking just to target advertisements is dangerous because that data can go to malicious actors. Just for accepting a cookie, you are more vulnerable to get an spam call or getting scammed because there is no moderation. 4. Talking about digital subscriptions, I only had one bad experience with a "x" VPN service. That made difficult to unsubscribe to the service, making me send an email just to disable the automatic re-subscription(Something that they apparently fixed when I searched years later). Adding to this problem, if the platform tries to gaslight you with messages, or using an excessive "are you sure?" pop-ups and buttons. They are praying on your feelings and is something that I hate. 5. Talking about the problematic commercial practices of social media influencers, I think this must also affect to public figures like football players or actors. Not some time ago, a lof of public figures and influencers, got away of scamming a lot of people because of the unregulated market of crypto currencies without any consequence. Public figures and influencers can also freely advertise gambling to minors with ease, something that I don't like. 6. I'm adding this extra point because this Digital Fairness Act, should also protect my online library of video-games. So if the service provider(Steam, GOG, Epic Games) ever stop their services I could still play them. Not only video-games, all the digital libraries from books, movies, software, etc...
